intro: Wrestling in Scripture carries both literal and metaphorical significance. The most theologically loaded instance is Jacob's nocturnal wrestling with the mysterious man at the ford of Jabbok (Genesis 32:22–32) — an encounter identified as divine (Genesis 32:30; Hosea 12:3–4) in which Jacob prevailed by clinging and would not let go until he received a blessing, and received both the blessing and a new name (Israel, "he who strives with God"). The episode established Jacob as the ancestor of a people defined by their striving with God, a pattern that shapes Israel's prayer life in the Psalms and prophets. Ephesians 6:12 uses the wrestling metaphor for spiritual conflict: "For we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness." Paul uses the athlete's vocabulary of intense bodily struggle to convey the seriousness and totality of the Christian's engagement with spiritual opposition, resolved not by human strength but by the full armour of God.
